==Diet and hunting==
[[File:Gallina de Guinea (Numida meleagris), parque nacional Kruger, Sudáfrica, 2018-07-25, DD 49.jpg|thumb|left|Gamebirds such as [[helmeted guineafowl]] are a favored part of the African hawk-eagle's diet.]]
The African hawk-eagle is an uncommonly aggressive and bold predator.<ref name= Steyn/> Their primary hunting tool is their powerful feet.<ref name= Simmons/> In general, their sneaky foraging techniques recall a very large [[Accipitrinae|accipitrine hawk]], especially in terms of their short-distance flight after prey.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hart |first1=Lorinda A |last2=Wreford |first2=Erin P |last3=Brown |first3=Mark |last4=Downs |first4=Colleen T |date=2018-07-03 |title=Hunting flight speeds of five southern African raptors |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.2989/00306525.2018.1455754 |journal=Ostrich |language=en |volume=89 |issue=3 |pages=251–258 |doi=10.2989/00306525.2018.1455754 |bibcode=2018Ostri..89..251H |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Mostly the African hawk-eagle engages in still-hunting, wherein they scan from prey from an inconspicuous perch for a long period. When prey is spotted, the hawk-eagle engages in a low level dash from their perch in cover.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/> They often uses cover to cloak their approach almost up the point where they strike.<ref name= Steyn/> While it has been claimed that their flight is "silent", it would presumably be more correct to say that the hunting hawk-eagle engages in minimal flapping flight so as to not alert prey to their approach.<ref name= Steyn/> Often, the hawk-eagles wait near prey-concentrated areas, such as waterholes and among riparian trees for birds to come to drink or by clearings that birds frequently cross.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> African hawk-eagles may also quarter above the ground and seize any prey they surprise.<ref name= Steyn/> They are capable of sometimes taking birds on the wing but usually prefer to catch them on the ground and may force avian prey intercepted in the air back to the ground. Often terrestrial birds are taken right around the moment they alight.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/> In some instances, prey may be chased, even on foot, into thickets.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/> African hawk-eagles have seldom been recorded engaging a stoop from soaring flight to capture prey.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Pairs of African hawk-eagles often hunt [[Pack hunter|together]] and in these circumstances seem to engage in deliberate cooperation, one distracting, the other striking.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Middleton/> They share this tendency for hunting in pairs with their sister species, the Bonelli's eagle, which has been inferred to engage in tandem hunts possibly for sociosexual pairing reasons rather than for obtaining larger or more difficult prey, as is typically assumed of tandem-hunting raptors.<ref name= Watve>Watve, M.G., Sant, N.R. & Joshi, V. (1995). ''Why Bonelli's Eagles hunt in pair: an assessment of individual and paired hunting successes''. Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society, 91: 355-359.</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hector |first=Dean P. |date=1986-01-12 |title=Cooperative Hunting and its Relationship to Foraging Success and Prey Size in an Avian Predator |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1439-0310.1986.tb00915.x |journal=Ethology |language=en |volume=73 |issue=3 |pages=247–257 |doi=10.1111/j.1439-0310.1986.tb00915.x |bibcode=1986Ethol..73..247H |issn=0179-1613|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ellis |first1=David H. |last2=Bednarz |first2=James C. |last3=Smith |first3=Dwight G. |last4=Flemming |first4=Stephen P. |date=January 1993 |title=Social Foraging Classes in Raptorial Birds |journal=BioScience |volume=43 |issue=1 |pages=14–20 |doi=10.2307/1312102 |issn=0006-3568 |jstor=1312102|bibcode=1993BiSci..43...14E }}</ref> One tandem hunting pair of African hawk-eagles appeared to remarkably make use of a mesh fence to drive [[Helmeted guineafowl|guineafowl]] into in a cunning strategy to prevent their escape.<ref name= Middleton>Middleton, A. & Middleton, I. (2005). ''Adaptive hunting by African Hawk-eagles''. Honeyguide 51: 23.</ref> Yet another pair of African hawk-eagles appears to derive much of its prey by regularly and opportunistically perching in a tree holding a [[fruit bat]] colony.<ref>Louette, M. (1975). ''A fruit-bat as prey of the African Hawk-Eagle''. Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club 95:172-173.</ref>

A trained African-hawk eagles can habitually kill [[European hare]]s (''Lepus europaeus'') around {{convert|4080|g|lb|abbr=on}} in weight, and one eagles around {{convert|1420|g|lb|abbr=on}} kills a large male [[domestic cat]] (''Felis catus'') around {{convert|4300|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Steyn2/> However, it is clear that wild eagles are usually focusing on much smaller prey.<ref name= Steyn2/> The typical prey of African hawk-eagles tends to be medium to large-sized [[bird]]s and small-to-medium-sized [[mammal]]s. Less usual prey can include [[reptile]]s and [[insect]]s.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/> As much as 74–86% of the diet can be birds and as much as 54–70% of the diet can be mammals.<ref name= Davison/> In general, due to a combination of factors such as their usual practice of keeping their nest area clean and their tendency to often evade researchers' notice due to hidden perch sites, the diet of African hawk-eagles is less well-known than that of larger African eagles or the Bonelli's eagles (especially in Europe).<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Kinahan>Kinahan, J. (1974). ''Observations on the Breeding of the African hawk eagle''. Mittitungen Ornithologische Arbeitsgrup, 6: 1-7.</ref> However, what is known suggests that this is an exceptionally powerful predator that may nearly rival much larger eagles such as [[martial eagle]]s (''Polemaetus bellicosus'') in its ability to take large and middle-sized prey.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Brown2/> A general picture has emerged that the African hawk-eagle is highly opportunistic and takes any variety of appropriately sized prey as it becomes available.<ref name= Steyn/> When the habitat is locally [[Overgrazing|overgrazed]] such as in Namibia, data suggests birds decrease in importance and mammals increase.<ref name= Steyn/> When taking mammalian prey, they may select any mammal weighing usually more than {{convert|300|g|oz|abbr=on}} of up to {{convert|4000|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Largish ground feeding birds, such as [[francolin]]s, [[Pternistis|spurfowl]]s and [[guineafowl]]s as well as mostly the smaller species of [[bustard]]s and [[hornbill]]s, seem to be the primary prey of African hawk-eagle.<ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Smeenk/> In [[Esigodini]] in Zimbabwe, it was found that 74% of the diet was birds. 69% of avian prey were found to be [[Galliformes|galliforms]] especially the [[helmeted guineafowl]] (''Numida meleagris''), which is of similar body size to the hawk-eagle itself, and [[Swainson's spurfowl]] (''Pternistis swainsonii''), which is about half the size of a hawk-eagle, in addition to three more francolins and spurfowls.<ref name= CRC/><ref name= Steyn2/> In the [[Matobo National Park|Matobo Hills]] of Zimbabwe and surrounding hilly country, the main prey by number were [[Natal spurfowl]] (''Pternistis natalensis'') at 48% of the diet but the main prey by [[biomass]] was [[hyrax]]es, at 51.3% (making up 29% by number) compared to the spurfowl which comprised 32.8% of the biomass.<ref name= Davison>Davison, B. (1998). ''Raptor communities in hill habitats in south-eastern Zimbabwe'' (Doctoral dissertation, Rhodes University).</ref> Both Zimbabwe studies shows that African hawk-eagles would habitually take mammalian prey larger than itself, including [[scrub hare]]s (''Lepus saxatilis'' ) of an estimated average of {{convert|2250|g|lb|abbr=on}}, and [[yellow-spotted rock hyrax]] (''Heterohyrax brucei'') and rarely [[Cape hyrax]] (''Procavia capensis''), of around {{convert|2000|g|lb|abbr=on}} with weights of infrequently up to {{convert|3000|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Davison/> Large prey was primary in [[Tsavo East National Park]], where the main prey was [[Kirk's dik-dik]] (''Madoqua kirkii''), at 35% of the diet. Due to the low volume of nest that could be found and studied, only a few nests were examined for African hawk-eagles in Tsavo East and in these it was found that they principally took young dik-diks. Overall, the hawk-eagles estimated to take around 68 dik-diks annually in the park overall. Secondary prey in Tsavo East were [[red-crested korhaan]] (''Lophotis ruficrista'') (at 12.7%), [[common dwarf mongoose]] (''Helogale parvula'') (at 9.2%) and [[unstriped ground squirrel]] (''Xerus rutilus'') and [[eastern yellow-billed hornbill]] (''Tockus flavirostris'') (both at 7.2%).<ref name= Smeenk/> At a small sample from a nest in Namibia, 42% of the diet consisted of [[meerkat]] (''Suricata suricatta'').<ref name= Kinahan/> In the [[North West (South African province)|Northwest province]] of South Africa,  the main prey were [[rock dove]]s (''Columba livia'') (23.4%), Natal spurfowl (14%), 6.5% by unidentified doves and [[bush squirrel]]s and 4.8% by Cape hyrax and [[crested francolin]]s (''Ortygornis sephaena'').<ref name= Franklin>Franklin, B. (2018). ''Observations, considerations and nesting behavior of the African Hawk Eagle''. [ONLINE]. Available at: http://safalconry.org/2016/05/20/observations-{{Dead link|date=August 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} considerations-and-nesting-behavior-of-the-african-hawk-eagle/?i=2.</ref> In [[Botswana]], the main prey appeared to be hornbills, with one nest found to contain 25 [[southern yellow-billed hornbill]]s (''Tockus leucomelas'') and only 5 [[southern red-billed hornbill]]s  (''Tockus rufirostris'') while elsewhere in the [[Zambezi Escarpment]], the red-billed hornbills dominated the diet.<ref name= Steyn/>

A small variety of different kinds of birds has been known to be taken opportunistically by African hawk-eagles.<ref name= Steyn/> At times, they will prey on colonial nests of birds from [[Ploceidae|weaver]]s such as [[red-billed quelea]]s (''Quelea quelea'') to [[heron]]s.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Seemingly infrequently taken birds have been known to include [[ostrich]] chicks, [[African pygmy goose|geese]], [[Three-banded plover|plovers]], [[White-browed coucal|cuckoos]], [[Black-headed heron|herons]] and [[Little egret|egrets]], [[Sacred ibis|ibises]], and a small diversity of [[passerine]]s.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= GRIN/><ref name= Franklin/> Assorted birds taken with some frequency include [[grey go-away-bird]]s (''Crinifer concolor''), [[brown-necked parrot]]s (''Poicephalus fuscicollis'') and several species of [[dove]].<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref>Perrin, M. (2012). ''Parrots of Africa, Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands: biology, ecology and conservation''. NYU Press.</ref> Domestic [[chicken]]s (''Gallus gallus domesticus'') are sometimes taken in cultivated areas by African hawk-eagles, but the species is not thought to be as much of a [[poultry]] killer as it is made out to be.<ref name= Steyn/> Other assorted mammals include a few species of [[squirrel]]s and [[rat]]s as well as [[Straw-coloured fruit bat|fruit bats]], [[Mohol bushbaby|bushbabies]], and even those as small as [[Cape short-eared gerbil]] (''Desmodillus auricularis'').<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Davison/><ref name= Franklin/><ref name= Kingdon>Kingdon, J., Happold, D., Butynski, T., Hoffmann, M., Happold, M., & Kalina, J. (2013). ''Mammals of Africa''. A&C Black.</ref> Larger mammals may include assorted species of [[mongoose]] to as large as the [[banded mongoose]] (''Mungos mungo'') and [[hare]] to as large as scrub hares, in many cases these may be as heavy or heavier than the hawk-eagle itself.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= GRIN/><ref name= Kingdon/> Other large mammalian prey can include [[South African springhare]]s (''Pedetes capensis'') and even small [[vervet monkey]]s (''Chlorocebus pygerythrus'').<ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Kingdon/><ref name= Dowsett>Dowsett, R. J., Aspinwall, D. R., & Dowsett-Lemaire, F. (2008). ''The Birds of Zambia: an Atlas and Handbook''. Tauraco Press.</ref> Besides smaller [[antelope]]s like [[dik-dik]]s, the young of a few antelope are known to have been attacked by African hawk-eagles: [[klipspringer]]s (''Oreotragus oreotragus''), [[steenbok]]s (''Raphicerus campestris'') and [[lechwe]]s (''Kobus leche''), the latter prey weighing an estimated {{convert|5000|g|lb|abbr=on}} and so perhaps the largest prey credited to the African hawk-eagle (although occasional adult dik-diks of up to a similar weight may also be attacked).<ref name= Kingdon/><ref name= Unwin>Unwin, M., & Tipling, D. (2018). ''The Empire of the Eagle: An Illustrated Natural History''. Yale University Press.</ref><ref>Estes, R. D. (2012). ''The Behavior Guide to African mammals: including hoofed mammals, carnivores, primates''. Univ of California Press.</ref> Predation on reptiles seems to be fairly uncommon and few such prey are definitively identified, but both [[Colubridae|colubrid]] [[snake]]s and [[cobra]]s are known to be included in their prey spectrum as well as [[lizard]]s such as [[chameleon]]s and [[giant plated lizard]]s (''Gerrhosaurus validus'').<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Franklin/> One impressive reptilian prey item credited to an African hawk-eagle was a fairly large [[African rock python]] (''Python sebae'') seemingly taken alive, which would rival the largest mammalian prey in weight.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Dowsett/> Occasionally, this species is credited with insect predation, usually by juveniles, but it is not clear what variety of insects are consumed. Such prey must be considered very rare and may often be secondarily ingested from the stomachs of prey such as rodents and game birds.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Davison/> The African hawk-eagle is rarely reported to [[Scavenger|scavenge]] on [[carrion]] but one pair was seen to repeatedly feed on the carcass of a [[southern reedbuck]] (''Redunca arundinum'') over 3 days.<ref name= Steyn/>
 
===Interspecific predatory relationships===
[[File:African Hawk Eagle (6608154801).jpg|thumb|left|An African hawk-eagle surveys its surroundings. This species tends to be aggressive towards larger raptors.]]
The African hawk-eagle exists in a highly competitive region for birds of prey.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/> They are obligated to share prey with both smaller and larger raptorial birds and have overlapping habitat areas with them as well.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Smeenk/><ref name= Davison/> In Tsavo East National Park, 50% of prey was shared with [[tawny eagle]]s (''Aquila rapax''), 37% with [[bateleur]] (''Terathopius ecaudatus'') and 54% with [[martial eagle]]s. Tawny eagles and bateleurs are about 25% larger in body mass than the African hawk-eagle while the martial eagle can be around three times larger than the hawk-eagle.<ref name= Smeenk/> The African hawk-eagle stands as the only of the four focused-on eagles in Tsavo East to nest apart from the other eagles due to habitat use, since it is the only one to use woodland over more lightly treed savanna, probably mitigating the most fierce of the competition. All four eagles mostly preyed on Kirk's dik-diks in Tsavo East but also their nesting periods were slightly staggered with the bateleur nesting rather earlier in the year than the others on average and the African hawk-eagle on average nesting the latest, so that the primary pressure on dik-dik prey was naturally staggered.<ref name= Smeenk/>  In the hill country of Zimbabwe, similarly the findings were that highly diverged nesting areas were used compared to other eagles, namely the primarily forest-nesting [[crowned eagle]] and primarily [[Rock (geology)|rock-nesting]] [[Verreaux's eagle]]. All three eagles hunted hyraxes in the hilly areas for primary prey by weight, supplemented by young [[antelope]]s for crowned eagles and gamebirds (which were primary in number) for African hawk-eagles. However, the larger eagles (both more than twice as heavy than the hawk-eagle) primarily took hyraxes taken that were usually rather bigger than those taken by hawk-eagle, normally being at least {{convert|2000|to|3000|g|lb|abbr=on}} for the crowned and the Verreaux's while that was the very largest sizes hunted by the hawk-eagles. The African hawk-eagle was found to have the most diverse diet of the larger raptors of the region. Despite its staggered nesting areas from other eagles, it was evidenced in the hill country of Zimbabwe that the larger raptors such as the crowned and Verreaux's eagles are attacked rather frequently, usually during flying bouts near the territories of the African hawk-eagles.<ref name= Davison/> These findings are consistent with elsewhere, showing that larger eagles are commonly attacked by the hawk-eagles, possibly either for competitive or [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbing]] purposes.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/> The predators for the African hawk-eagle are not known and studies have indicated that adult hawk-eagles have little to fear and may fulfill the niche of an [[apex predator]], albeit one that focuses on smaller prey typically than the considerably larger eagles it co-exists with.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Davison/> Predation on other birds of prey is rarely documented, unlike with Bonelli's eagles, but the African hawk-eagle has been known to hunt down [[Western barn owl|barn owl]]s (''Tyto alba'') and [[black-winged kite]]s (''Elanus caeruleus'').<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Davison/><ref name= Franklin/>

==Behaviour and breeding==
[[File:Aigle fascié MHNT.jpg|thumb|{{center|''Aquila spilogaster'' egg}}]]
The African hawk-eagle tends to be solitary but adults frequently occur in pairs, perhaps spending more time together than is typical of many raptors.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/> Breeding territories are established with aerial displays, which are fairly uninvolved compared to some related species.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Brown2/> The displays are usually little more than mutual circling with intermittent calling but males sometimes uncommonly do engage in sky dances, probably only in breeding displays rather than territorial exclusion displays. During the sky dance they engage in relatively shallow undulations with steep plunges and short rises with little wing flapping. In many cases, the male will dive towards the female and the female, in turn, turns to the male and displays her claws. This mating ritual culminates in the male presenting the female with nuptial gifts in the form of prey.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/> This species is typically highly monogamous and it is quite typical for adults to pair for life.<ref name= Steyn/> However, a potential case of [[Polygyny in animals|polygyny]] may have occurred in Ethiopia.<ref name= Ash/> The breeding season occurs from October to April in the north of the [[Equator]], while it is in February–June in The Gambia.<ref name= Ash/><ref name= Ash2/> In [[East Africa]], the nesting season is often into April–January.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Smeenk/> In [[Uganda]], egg laying was reported in September to November with a juvenile bird seen by January.<ref name= GRIN/> Down to Botswana and northeastern South Africa the nesting season is somewhat variable, recorded from April to October, with egg-laying peaking in June according to one authority.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/><ref>Irwin, M. P. S. (1981). ''The Birds of Zimbabwe''. Quest Pub.</ref><ref name= Dowsett-Lemaire>Dowsett-Lemaire, F. & Dowsett, R. J. (2006). ''The Birds of Malawi. An Atlas and Handbook''. Tauraco Press.</ref> Nesting is ultimately timed to line up with the regional [[dry season]].<ref name= BOTW/>

===Nest===
The nest tends to be a very substantial platform-like structure of large sticks. It is typically located in the main fork or well out on a lateral branch of a large tree.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> The height of the nest has been recorded as being from {{convert|4|to|36|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground, generally falling somewhere between {{convert|9|and|13|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> In southern Africa, a nest at a height of {{convert|4.2|m|ft|abbr=on}} was considered unusually low.<ref name= Steyn/> Common tree species used as nests are ''[[Acacia]]'', ''[[Adansonia]]'', ''[[Khaya]]'', ''[[Terminalia (plant)|Terminalia]]'' and non-native ''[[Eucalyptus]]''. Quite often, the nest trees are located in riparian areas.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= GRIN/><ref name= Dowsett-Lemaire/><ref>Dowsett-Lemaire, F. (2006). ''An annotated list and life history of the birds of Nyika National Park, Malawi-Zambia''. Tauraco Res. Rep, 8, 1-64.</ref> Rarely to exceptionally, nests may be placed elsewhere other than a tree.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> This seems to be case generally in East Africa where rare nests have been documented in a [[Shrub|bush]] or on cliff ledge.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> In southern Africa sometimes nests have been placed on pylons.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Snelling |first=John C. |date=1 Jan 1975 |title=Endangered birds of prey: ideas on the management of some African species |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10520/AJA03794369_3091 |journal=South African Journal of Wildlife Research |volume=5 |issue=1 |pages=27–31|hdl=10520/AJA03794369_3091 }}</ref> Nesting locations typically provide some shade but some nest sites are rather exposed, necessitating the shading of the eaglet by the female, even to a period longer than the nesting period.<ref name= Steyn/> The nest itself is typically from {{convert|50|to|80|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep or sometimes more, sometimes over {{convert|125|cm|in|abbr=on}} with repeated additions.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> The interior cup of the nest is about {{convert|25|to|30|cm|in|abbr=on}} with the overall nest diameter is up to about {{convert|100|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Steyn/> Nests repairs consists of building up the rim after the previous years flattened, reinforcing by adding new layers of sticks.<ref name= Steyn/> Building takes about 4 to 5 weeks and sometimes repairs as long as 8 weeks.<ref name= Steyn/> It make take several months for a new nest's construction.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Brown2/> Limited observation suggests the male mate takes primary role in nest repair but the female may be more active in new nest construction.<ref name= Steyn/> Females may add a majority of the fresh green leaves recorded in nests.<ref name= Steyn/> The species tends to prefer a narrow breeding area with favorable habitat for nesting site, and in one extreme case the same general [[Grove (nature)|grove]] near [[Pretoria]] was used by different generations of hawk-eagles from 1912 to 1978.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/> In Zimbabwe bushveld two nests were found {{convert|12|km|mi|abbr=on}} apart, in [[Kruger National Park]] nests were estimated to be {{convert|5|km|mi|abbr=on}} apart and in the well-wooded areas of the Matobos, nests were only {{convert|3|km|mi|abbr=on}} apart.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Davison/>

===Eggs and incubation===
The African hawk-eagle usually lays a clutch of 2 eggs. More infrequently, a clutch of 1 to 3 eggs may be laid.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> In Zambia, 46% of nests had one egg, the remainder all had 2 in a sample of 13 nests.<ref name= Dowsett/> However, in Malawi, 80% of 15 nests had two eggs with only the remaining 20% having one.<ref name=  Dowsett-Lemaire/>  As of the early 1980s, only one recorded nest in southern Africa was said to contain a 3 egg clutch.<ref name= Steyn/> The eggs have a chalky white ground color with very variable markings, varying from handsomely blotched speckled with dull rust-red to quite plain to showing coalesced markings on either end.<ref name= Steyn/> The eggs can measure {{convert|59.5|to|75.2|mm |in|abbr=on}} in height, averaging {{convert|64.5|mm|in|abbr=on}}, by {{convert|46|to|55.7|mm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter,  averaging {{convert|51.3|mm|in|abbr=on}}, in sample of 123. Only particularly large eggs are said to measure over {{convert|70|mm|in|abbr=on}} high.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Roberts/> The weight of eggs is reported {{convert|75|to|100|g|oz|abbr=on}}, averaging some {{convert|87|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Heyman/> The eggs in a multiple egg clutch are laid at about 3 or 4 day intervals, with the incubation beginning with the first egg laying.<ref name= Steyn/> The incubation stage lasts for 42-44.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> The female does most of the incubation, relieved only for short spells by males who usually bring prey for her. Male incubation can be as long as an hour but usually much shorter. Over 28 hours of observation over 9 days at 2 nests, the females of the two pairs incubated a mean of 82.6% of the time to the males' 7.1%, while the remainder no incubation.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/> Green leaves are still added by both members of the pair during incubation.<ref name= Steyn/>

===Nesting and fledging stages===
A chick African hawk-eagle may take just under 2 days to hatch. Newly hatched eaglets are covered in dark grey down with whitish down on abdomen and thighs, with dull yellow cere and feet. The first coat of grey gives way to thicker and whiter second coat, by 2 weeks only head and back have any grey down. By 3 weeks down predominantly white only head grey, with first remiges breaking out of quills. Feather development rapid at a month old and by 5 weeks well feathered below. At 6 weeks, the eaglet will only have remaining down on head, crop and abdomen, fully feathered by a week later but for wings and tails.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/> The weight of 2 days old eaglets is about {{convert|80|g|oz|abbr=on}}, about {{convert|200|g|oz|abbr=on}} at 9 days, {{convert|740|g|lb|abbr=on}} at 21 days, {{convert|950|g|lb|abbr=on}} at 28 days, {{convert|1135|g|lb|abbr=on}} at 35 days, and {{convert|1250|g|lb|abbr=on}} at 49 days.<ref name= Heyman/><ref>McLachlan, G. R. & Liversidge, R. (1978). ''Roberts Birds of South Africa''. John Voelcker Bird Book Fund: Cape Town.</ref> In terms of developmental growth, at 5 days old, nestling African hawk-eagles are just barely able to preen themselves and by 11 days can move slightly around the nest. Young eaglets spend a great deal of the day sleeping and most awake activity involves preening and feeding. Only at the age of 24 days can the chicks defend the nest, stand reasonably well and make a few rather clumsy wing exercises; however at this stage they cannot tear meat off of the food that a parent provide. When the eaglet is 32 days old it is mainly attended to for feeding, stands well and exercises wings. At 50 days of age, the chicks show signs of fledging through being able to feed themselves and through flapping their wings. Around this stage they may preen quite a lot, nibble on sticks of the nest and make mock kills of prey bones and of sticks. Some of these activities are said to improve coordination. Fledgling begins at between 60 and 70 days of age, reports of as little as 41 days for fledgling are probably dubious. Post-nesting attachment to parental care is not long for a tropical raptor, typically lasting about 3 to 4 weeks. Thereafter, the young African hawk-eagle may be seen farther afield but then again some are seen in the company of their parents for as long as 2 months after fledging.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/>

A 2008 study found that the first-born chick in a nest will crush, acquire more food than, and almost inevitably kill the second, smaller chick.<ref name= Meyburg>{{Cite journal|last=Meyburg|first=Bernd-Ulrich|date=2008-04-03 |title= ''Sibling aggression and mortality among nestling eagles'' | journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=116 |issue=2 |pages=224–228 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1974.tb00243.x |issn=0019-1019}}</ref> The smaller chick typically has little chance of survival given the size differences of the two. In one case, the younger one weighed {{convert|62|g|oz|abbr=on}} upon hatching, when its sibling was already {{convert|142|g|oz|abbr=on}}, in another case the weights at the corresponding ages were {{convert|64|g|oz|abbr=on}} and {{convert|111|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Steyn/> This is a common occurrence in many bird species, particularly predatory birds, which allows for a chick to thrive and in the unlikely event that the first chick dies, there will be the second chick to propagate the species.<ref name= Meyburg/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Simmons |first=Robert |date=July 1988 |title=Offspring quality and the evolution of cainism |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1988.tb00992.x |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=130 |issue=4 |pages=339–357 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1988.tb00992.x |issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription }}</ref> According to a 1959 study, the instinct for two chicks to fight subsides after a few weeks thus if the second chick manages to survive for that long, the chances that it will [[fledge]] will be increased. The same paper suggests that intrabrood cannibalism likely follows a [[siblicide]] event.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ingram |first=Collingwood |date=1959 |title=''The Importance of Juvenile Cannibalism in the Breeding Biology of Certain Birds of Prey'' |jstor=4081778 |journal=The Auk |volume=76 |issue=2 |pages=218–226|doi=10.2307/4081778|doi-access=free }}</ref> No cases of two successfully raised young were documented in southern Africa by the 1980s but two fledglings have been claimed produced in about 20% of Kenyan nests.<ref name= Steyn/> The reasons for the discrepancies in the two regions is not well-understood.<ref name= Steyn/>

===Parental behaviour===
[[File:African hawk eagles (Aquila spilogaster) 2.jpg|thumb|left|A likely pair in [[Damaraland]], [[Namibia]]. African hawk-eagles appear to spend much time in pairs.]]
Parental attention may be loosely divided into three main stages: from hatching up to 2 weeks, from 2–4 weeks and from 4 weeks to fledging.<ref name= Steyn/> During the first period, the female largely remains on nest and broods a great deal, while, as at most stages, the male provides prey but then again has been known to brood and feed a small eaglet. Upon the 2nd week, the eaglet(s) are not nearly as closely brooded. In the second 2-4 week period, the female is at nest much less but she usually perches in nest tree, male still provides much of the prey and the female continues to feed, and shade if necessary, the eaglet. During the 3rd stage, parental time at nest drops much farther and the female may still sleep on the nest overnight but possibly apart from eaglet. Only at very late stage does female start to catch prey for eaglet.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/> Nests tend to be kept very clear; with female known to disperse pellets elsewhere and much avian prey is brought well plucked, making prey identification difficult.<ref name= Steyn/> The parent African hawk-eagles are known to highly aggressive in protecting their nest and regularly dive on threatening or novel animals who approach the nest.<ref name= Steyn2/> Humans are not infrequently attacked around the nest and especially if climbing towards the nest, resulting in potentially painful injuries.<ref name= Steyn/> The species ranks as perhaps one of the most aggressive African eagles in defending its nest against humans, alongside the crowned eagle, and is much more prone to diving at humans than nesting Bonelli's eagles.<ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Davison/>

===Breeding success and dispersal===
In a study conducted in Zimbabwe in 1988, 116 African hawk-eagle pairs were assessed in terms of breeding success in two areas of varying substrate quality. It was found that nests were placed in flat-crowned thorn trees in areas with basaltic soils and round-crowned, rough-barked trees in areas with sandy soil. Rainfall affected breeding success, laying dates and the sizes of clutches with higher success, later laying dates and larger clutch sizes correlating with higher rainfall. More breeding attempts were made in open woodland areas than in closed however the number of resulting chicks did not differ between vegetation structure.<ref name= Hustler/> The African hawk-eagle is usually a rather sedentary and stable breeding raptor, seldom leaving a devoted area holding good prey numbers and habitat.<ref name= Roberts/> However, one rather unsuccessful pair was recorded to have used 4 different sites in 9 years, atypical behavior for the species.<ref name= Steyn/> Usually this species is able to breed annually, unlike many other African eagles.<ref name= Brown2/> For instance, at a nest site in Zimbabwe there only 2 non breeding years in 17 years, productivity overall was 0.82 young per nest per year. Other nests in Zimbabwe often failed and egg infertility was a problem, mean overall productivity was 0.54.<ref name= Steyn2/> In Kenya, 15 young were produced in 27 pair years, productivity of 0.56 young per pair per year.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Simmons/> The mortality of young African hawk-eagles, i.e. under 4 years old, may be extremely high in some localities, up to 75%, mainly due to assorted [[Human impact on the environment|anthropogenic causes]] such as [[persecution]] and collisions with manmade objects such as wires.<ref name= Heyman/> Despite the reputation for being sedentary, according to a 2006 book, juveniles of this species can disperse considerably from the breeding grounds.<ref>Bildstein, K. L. (2006). ''Migrating Raptors of the World: their Ecology & Conservation''. Cornell University Press.</ref> The mean distance for African hawk-eagles from their ringing as nestlings to recovery was {{convert|25|km|mi|abbr=on}} in southern Africa.<ref>Oatley, T., Oschadleus, H.D., Novarro, R. A., Underhill, L. G. (1998). ''Review of ring recoveries of birds of prey in Southern Africa, 1948-1998''. Avian Demography Unit, Department of Statistical Sciences, University of Cape Town.</ref> Similarly an adult hawk-eagle found dead in the Matobos was only {{convert|15|km|mi|abbr=on}} away from its natal site.<ref name= Steyn/> However, one ringed hawk-eagle was found to have moved a distance of {{convert|795 |km|mi|abbr=on}} from [[Limpopo]] in South Africa to [[Victoria Falls]] on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border, perhaps as a response to a prolonged [[Drought|dry spell]] and resulting diminishing food resources.<ref name= Simmons/><ref name= GRIN/>

==Status==
The African hawk-eagle has a very wide range and is a relatively common species. No particular threats have been identified but the population is thought to be declining slowly. The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] has rated its conservation status as being of "[[Least-concern species|least concern]]".<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> [[BirdLife International]] estimated the total number of adults at around 100,000 birds, but that the supporting data are poor. In the former [[Transvaal (province)|Transvaal province]] of South Africa the population of hawk-eagles was thought to be about 1,600 pairs, while overall in the southern African region an extrapolated total number of about 7,000 pairs was estimated.<ref name= Simmons/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tarboton |first1=WR |last2=Allan |first2=D.G. |date=1984 |title=The status and conservation of birds of prey in the Transvaal |url=https://journals.co.za/doi/abs/10.10520/AJA090799001_88 |journal=Transvaal Museum Monographs |volume=3 |issue=1}}</ref> A 2006 study found that the African hawk eagle, among other raptor species have been declining at high rates outside of protected areas in West Africa and only seem to be stabilizing through the efforts of national parks.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Thiollay |first=Jean-Marc |date=2006-04-13|title=''The decline of raptors in West Africa: long-term assessment and the role of protected areas''|journal=Ibis|language=en|volume=148|issue=2|pages=240–254|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.2006.00531.x|issn=0019-1019}}</ref> When numbers from 2003 to 2004 are compared to 1969-1973 in West Africa, it is found that numbers have declined even in protected areas.<ref>Rondeau, G., & Thiollay, J. M. (2004). ''West African vulture decline''. Vulture news, 51, 13-33.</ref> Overall negative population trends have also been detected for some time in southern Africa.<ref name= Steyn/><ref>Benson, C. W., & Benson, F. M. (1977). ''The Birds of Malaŵi''. Montfort Press.</ref> Despite claims that declines in Malawi are due to [[persecution]] of the African hawk-eagle as a poultry thief, the much stronger cause is likely to be the pervasive [[Habitat destruction|destruction]] of woodlands.<ref name= Dowsett-Lemaire/> The declines are strong enough in southern Africa for the species to be thought to be extinct as breeding in Eswatini.<ref name= Monadjem/><ref>Monadjem, A. (2003). ''Threatened vertebrates of Swaziland: Swaziland red data book: fishes, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals''. Ministry of Tourism, Environment and Communications.</ref>

zh.extract:
非洲隼雕（學名：Aquila spilogaster）是一種猛禽，牠們一般會在多樹木的山上出現，在樹木的交叉枝幹上建造一個直徑大約1米的鳥巢。
非洲隼雕身長55到68公分，上半身呈黑色。牠們會狩獵哺乳動物、爬行動物及鳥類，牠們會發出尖銳的kluu-kluu-kluu的叫聲。
